message:
perf(core): precompute full GF256 multiplication table at compile time

`gf256_mul_table(coeff)` was synthesizing a 256-byte product row
dynamically on every call. The hot path through RaptorQ symbol mix
and GF(256) `addmul` operations hits this function repeatedly during
encoding/decoding work, producing the same 256 outputs for the same
input coefficient every time. Each invocation paid for an in-place
loop calling `gf256_mul_byte` 256 times — and `gf256_mul_byte` itself
does the bit-walk-with-XOR-reduction over the 0x1D field polynomial,
so the per-byte cost was non-trivial.

Move the table to a static built at compile time:

  - `gf256_mul_byte_for_table(a, b)` is a `const fn` clone of the
    runtime `gf256_mul_byte` shape (bit-walk multiplication with the
    AES-style 0x1D irreducible polynomial reduction). The clone is
    needed because the runtime version is non-`const`; rather than
    cascade a const conversion through all callers, declare a parallel
    helper that's only used by the table builder.
  - `gf256_mul_row(coeff)` returns the 256-byte row of products for a
    single coefficient, also `const fn`. Uses an explicit `loop` with
    `byte == u8::MAX` break instead of a range so it stays const-eval
    friendly.
  - `gf256_mul_tables()` walks coefficients 0..=255 the same way and
    returns the full `[[u8; 256]; 256]` table.
  - `static GF256_MUL_TABLES` materializes the result. 64 KiB total
    (256 × 256 bytes); the const evaluator builds it during compile.
    `#[allow(clippy::large_stack_arrays)]` is correct — the lint
    misreads this as a runtime stack allocation, but it's a `static`
    initializer that lives in `.rodata`.
  - `gf256_mul_table(coeff)` now returns `&'static [u8; 256]` (was
    `[u8; 256]` by value), pointing into the precomputed table. All
    existing callers borrow the row read-only, so the reference
    return is the right shape and avoids a 256-byte copy per call.

Net effect: per-call cost drops from "compute 256 GF(256) products
into a stack-resident array, return by move" to "one static index +
return reference". The encoding/decoding hot paths see this cost
disappear entirely (compile-time evaluation), and the binary takes
on a fixed +64 KiB rodata footprint that compiles down further when
the table is partially monomorphized away by callers that only need
specific rows.
